home.title: The Nanosoft Package
home.subtitle: Interactive tools for exploring Low Energy Nuclear Reactions (LENR) and cold fusion transmutation pathways
home.basedOn: Based on the groundbreaking work of Dr. Alexander Parkhomov

queryReactions.title: Query Nuclear Reactions
queryReactions.description: Search through thousands of exothermic fusion, fission, and two-to-two nuclear transmutation reactions

cascadeSimulations.title: Cascade Simulations
cascadeSimulations.description: Model chain reactions and predict reaction products from cascading LENR processes

elementData.title: Element & Nuclide Data
elementData.description: Browse comprehensive tables of nuclear and chemical properties

advancedQueries.title: Advanced Queries
advancedQueries.description: Execute custom SQL queries across all reaction and property tables

parkhomovTables.title: About the Parkhomov Tables
parkhomovTables.intro: This package is based on the pioneering work of Russian physicist Dr. Alexander Parkhomov, who published comprehensive tables of possible cold nuclear transmutation reactions in 2018.
parkhomovTables.analysisIntro: Using computer analysis of 280 stable nuclides, Dr. Parkhomov identified:
parkhomovTables.twoToTwoCount: 516,789 two-to-two reactions - transforming pairs of nuclides
parkhomovTables.exothermicNote: All reactions in these tables are exothermic (energy-producing) and represent thermodynamically favorable pathways for nuclear transmutation at low energies.
parkhomovTables.developers: Developed by R.W. Greenyer and P.W. Power for the Martin Fleischmann Memorial Project

originalApp.title: Original Application
originalApp.description: This is a modern reimplementation of the original Nanosoft Package, which was developed as a PHP web application to explore Dr. Parkhomov's nuclear transmutation tables.
originalApp.findAt: The original application and comprehensive documentation can be found at:
originalApp.gratitude: We are grateful to R. W. Greenyer and P. W. Power for their pioneering work in making this important research accessible.

openSource.title: Open Source Project
openSource.description: This project is open source and licensed under the GNU Affero General Public License v3.0 (AGPL-3.0). We welcome contributions, bug reports, and feature requests from the community.

elements.name: Name
elements.shellDesignations: Shell designations: K (innermost), L, M, N (outer shells)
elements.selectElementToView: Select an element from the periodic table above to view its properties and isotopes.
elements.clickElement: Click any element to see detailed information about its properties, isotopes, and nuclear data.
elements.isotopicRelative: Isotopic % is relative to all isotopes of {{element}}
elements.bosonFermionNuclear: Nuclear: Determined by mass number (A). Even A = Boson, Odd A = Fermion.
elements.bosonFermionAtomic: Atomic: Determined by neutron count (N = A - Z). Even N = Boson, Odd N = Fermion.
elements.bosonFermionQuantum: This classification affects quantum statistical behavior and reaction probabilities.

reactions.increaseLimit: Increase limit to see more results

reactions.fusionDescription: Query exothermic fusion reactions where two nuclei combine to form a heavier nucleus
reactions.fissionDescription: Query exothermic fission reactions where a nucleus splits into two lighter nuclei
reactions.twoToTwoDescription: Query exothermic two-to-two reactions where two nuclei transform into two different nuclei
reactions.heatmapDescription: Click any element to filter the results table and nuclides list to show only reactions containing that element. Color intensity shows the selected metric value.
reactions.frequencyDescription: Number of times each element appears across {{total}} reactions (as input or output). Higher frequency = darker color.
reactions.energyDescription: Total energy (MeV) from all reactions involving each element. Higher total energy = darker color.
reactions.diversityDescription: Number of unique isotopes of each element appearing in the results. More isotopic variety = darker color.
reactions.runQueryToView: Run a query to view {{type}} reactions.
reactions.noMatchingReactions: No reactions match the selected filter.
reactions.elementExistsInFull: Element {{element}} exists in the full dataset but not in the limited results.
reactions.clickForDetails: Click on an element or a nuclide above to see detailed properties

histogram.title: Energy Distribution
histogram.description: Histogram of reaction energy (MeV) values with statistical summary

russellChart.title: Russell Chart — Nine-Octave Periodic Table
russellChart.description: Walter Russell's wave-based arrangement of elements into 9 octaves with a serpentine pattern, showing generation (charging) and radiation (discharging) sides with inert gases at the balance points.
russellChart.clickToView: Click any known element to view its properties and isotopes in the database
russellChart.zoomControls: Scroll to zoom, drag to pan, double-click to zoom in
russellChart.waveDescription: Elements plotted as points on Russell's octave wave — amplitude rises through Generation (compression) to Carbon at the apex, then falls through Radiation (expansion)
russellChart.predictionsDescription: Walter Russell predicted several elements before their official discovery, demonstrating the predictive power of his wave-based periodic system.
russellChart.aboutParagraph1: Walter Russell (1871–1963) published his Nine-Octave Periodic Table in "The Universal One" (1926), arranging all elements into a wave-based system of octaves. Each octave has a generation (charging) side and a radiation (discharging) side, with an inert gas at the balance point — the fulcrum of the wave.
russellChart.aboutParagraph2: In Russell's cosmology, matter is created by opposing spiraling vortices of compression (generation) and expansion (radiation). Carbon sits at the amplitude — the point of maximum compression — making it the most balanced and stable element. The serpentine S-curve pattern shows how each octave reverses direction, creating a continuous wave from the lightest hypothetical substances to the heaviest known elements.
russellChart.aboutParagraph3: Russell notably predicted the existence of Deuterium (which he called "Ethlogen"), Tritium ("Bebegen"), Neptunium, and Plutonium years before their official laboratory discovery — validating his wave-based model's predictive capacity.
russellChart.view3DControls: Drag to rotate, scroll to zoom
russellChart.experimentalNote: This visualization is based on Russell's original 1926 chart. Russell's system differs significantly from the modern periodic table — it represents his philosophical and cosmological model of matter rather than the quantum mechanical model used in contemporary chemistry.

notes.hypotheticalFirstInertGas: Hypothetical first inert gas — the beginning of matter
notes.hypotheticalTransUranicInSystem: Hypothetical trans-uranic element in Russell's system
notes.hypotheticalFinalInertGas: Hypothetical final inert gas — the end of matter
notes.heliumPlacement: Russell placed Helium as the inert gas of octave 4
notes.carbonAmplitude: Carbon — the amplitude of the wave; maximum compression point
notes.neptuniumPrediction: Russell predicted element 93 in 1926 — discovered as Neptunium in 1940
notes.plutoniumPrediction: Russell predicted element 94 in 1926 — discovered as Plutonium in 1941

networkGraph.instructions: Drag nodes to reposition. Scroll to zoom.

segreChart.description: Interactive chart of nuclides plotted by proton number (Z) vs neutron number (N), color-coded by stability
segreChart.clickToView: Click any nuclide to view details
segreChart.zoomControls: Scroll to zoom, drag to pan, double-click to zoom in

cascades.title: Cascades
cascades.simulationDescription: Model cascading chain reactions from initial fuel nuclides
cascades.databaseLimitsDescription: The database contains 324 nuclides across 92 elements, with 3,921 fusion reactions and 1.2M two-to-two reactions. Slider ranges reflect these constraints. Default settings (5000 max nuclides, 25 loops) provide comprehensive results. Processing time ranges from 30 seconds to 15 minutes depending on parameters.
cascades.weightedModeDescription: Pathway frequencies will be weighted by fuel proportions using a hybrid deterministic/Monte Carlo approach. Results reflect the probability of reactions occurring based on relative abundances.
cascades.selectIsotopesLabel: Select specific isotopes for your fuel mixture
cascades.selectIsotopesHint: Click on elements to select specific isotopes. Color coding indicates natural abundance.
cascades.allowDimers: Allow Dimer Formation (H, N, O, F, Cl, Br, I)
cascades.noReactionsFoundDescription: No reactions were found matching your energy thresholds and fuel nuclides. Try adjusting your parameters or adding more fuel nuclides.
cascades.howStep1: Start with your specified fuel nuclides
cascades.howStep2: Find all possible Fusion and 2-2 reactions between these nuclides
cascades.howStep3: Products meeting energy/temperature criteria are "fed back" as new reactants
cascades.howStep4: Process repeats recursively up to the max loop count
cascades.howStep5: Results show the full cascade of reactions and final product distribution
cascades.materialsCatalogSubtitle: Select a material or save your current fuel mixture
cascades.selectMaterialToView: Select a material to view details
cascades.name: Name
cascades.saveCurrentFuelMixture: This will save your current fuel mixture ({{count}} nuclides) as a custom material.
cascades.tipFlowViewDescription: Use the Flow View tab to see a visual representation of the top pathways, or Pathway Browser to explore all pathways with sorting and filtering.
cascades.networkViewDescription: Network view shows hierarchical relationship between nuclides. For large cascades, the Flow View may be easier to read.
cascades.calculatingEnergyDescription: Computing total energy from all reactions...
cascades.searchingReactions: Searching for reactions between active nuclides and products...

sankey.title: Reaction Flow Diagram
sankey.flowDescription: This shows your cascade reactions flowing from left to right:
sankey.hoverToSee: over flows to see reaction details
sankey.noMatchingPathways: No pathways match current filters. Try adjusting your filter criteria.
sankey.highPathwayWarning: High pathway counts may cause slow rendering or errors
sankey.renderErrorDescription: The Sankey diagram encountered an error while rendering. This usually happens with too many pathways.
sankey.renderErrorHint: Try reducing the number of pathways or using filters to simplify the data.
sankey.flowWidthRaw: represents how often each reaction pathway occurs
sankey.legendHoverDesc: over flows to see detailed reaction information
sankey.legendToggleHint: Use the {{mode}} button to toggle between weighted and raw frequencies
